word: a bad workman always blames his tools
meaning: When someone performs a job poorly, they tend to blame their equipment rather than their own lack of skill.
example: He blamed the oven for the burnt cake, but a bad workman always blames his tools.

word: a bird in the hand is worth two in the bush
meaning: It is better to keep what you have than to risk losing it by trying to get something better.
example: I think I'll keep my current job rather than looking for a new one. A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

word: absence makes the heart grow fonder
meaning: When people are apart, their love for each other increases.
example: Since he moved to another city for work, they miss each other terribly. Absence makes the heart grow fonder.

word: a chain is only as strong as its weakest link
meaning: A team or organization is only as strong or successful as its weakest or least successful member.
example: We need to make sure everyone understands the plan, because a chain is only as strong as its weakest link.

word: a fool and his money are soon parted
meaning: Foolish people do not know how to hold on to their money and spend it carelessly.
example: He spent all his lottery winnings in a month. A fool and his money are soon parted.

word: a friend in need is a friend indeed
meaning: A true friend is one who helps you when you are in a difficult situation.
example: When I was sick, Sarah brought me soup every day. A friend in need is a friend indeed.

word: a journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step
meaning: Even the longest and most difficult tasks must be started with a small, first step.
example: Learning a new language is hard, but a journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.

word: a leopard cannot change its spots
meaning: People cannot change their basic nature or bad habits.
example: He promised he wouldn't be late again, but a leopard cannot change its spots.

word: all good things must come to an end
meaning: Nothing lasts forever; even pleasant experiences must eventually finish.
example: Our holiday was amazing, but all good things must come to an end.

word: all that glitters is not gold
meaning: Something that looks attractive or valuable on the outside may not be so on the inside.
example: The car looks shiny and new, but the engine is terrible. All that glitters is not gold.

word: all's well that ends well
meaning: If the outcome of a situation is happy, then any difficulties along the way do not matter.
example: We got lost on the way, but we found a great restaurant. All's well that ends well.

word: an apple a day keeps the doctor away
meaning: Eating healthy food helps you stay healthy and avoid getting sick.
example: Make sure you eat your fruits! An apple a day keeps the doctor away.

word: an empty vessel makes much noise
meaning: People who lack knowledge or intelligence often talk the most and the loudest.
example: He boasts a lot about his skills, but an empty vessel makes much noise.

word: an idle brain is the devil's workshop
meaning: People who have nothing to do are more likely to get into trouble or do bad things.
example: You should find a hobby; an idle brain is the devil's workshop.

word: an ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure
meaning: It is easier and better to stop a problem from happening than to fix it after it has occurred.
example: Always wear your seatbelt. An ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure.

word: a picture is worth a thousand words
meaning: A single image can express complex ideas more effectively than a long description.
example: I can't explain how beautiful the sunset was, but a picture is worth a thousand words.

word: a rolling stone gathers no moss
meaning: A person who constantly moves from place to place never accumulates wealth or lasting relationships.
example: He changes jobs every few months. A rolling stone gathers no moss.

word: a stitch in time saves nine
meaning: Fixing a small problem immediately will prevent it from becoming a much larger problem later.
example: Mend that tear in your shirt now before it gets bigger. A stitch in time saves nine.

word: as you sow, so you shall reap
meaning: You will eventually have to face the consequences of your actions.
example: He was always rude to others, and now he has no friends. As you sow, so you shall reap.

word: barking dogs seldom bite
meaning: People who make a lot of threats rarely carry them out.
example: Don't be afraid of his yelling; barking dogs seldom bite.

word: beauty is in the eye of the beholder
meaning: Different people have different ideas about what is beautiful.
example: I don't like her dress, but beauty is in the eye of the beholder.

word: beauty is only skin deep
meaning: A person's character and personality are more important than their physical appearance.
example: He is very handsome but unkind. Beauty is only skin deep.

word: beggars can't be choosers
meaning: When you rely on the kindness of others, you should accept whatever is offered to you.
example: I wanted the blue pen, but they only had black. Oh well, beggars can't be choosers.

word: better late than never
meaning: It is better to do something after it was supposed to be done than not to do it at all.
example: The train arrived an hour late, but better late than never.

word: better safe than sorry
meaning: It is better to be careful now than to take a risk and regret it later.
example: I'll take my umbrella even though it's sunny. Better safe than sorry.

word: birds of a feather flock together
meaning: People with similar interests or characteristics tend to spend time with each other.
example: Those two love playing chess, so they became fast friends. Birds of a feather flock together.

word: blood is thicker than water
meaning: Family relationships are stronger and more important than other relationships.
example: When he was in trouble, his friends left, but his brother stayed. Blood is thicker than water.

word: cleanliness is next to godliness
meaning: Being clean is extremely important and a good habit.
example: Wash your hands before dinner. Remember, cleanliness is next to godliness.

word: cowards die many times before their deaths
meaning: People who are afraid constantly suffer from fear, whereas brave people only suffer when they actually die.
example: Don't be afraid to try out for the play. Cowards die many times before their deaths.

word: cross the stream where it is shallowest
meaning: To choose the easiest way to do something.
example: Let's start with the easiest homework first. Cross the stream where it is shallowest.

word: curiosity killed the cat
meaning: Being too inquisitive can get you into trouble.
example: Don't look into that box. Curiosity killed the cat.

word: don't bite the hand that feeds you
meaning: Do not treat poorly those who help you or provide for you.
example: You shouldn't complain about your parents' rules; don't bite the hand that feeds you.

word: don't count your chickens before they hatch
meaning: Do not make plans based on a good event that has not yet happened.
example: You haven't won the competition yet, so don't count your chickens before they hatch.

word: don't cry over spilt milk
meaning: There is no point in being upset about a mistake or loss that has already happened and cannot be fixed.
example: You broke the vase, but don't cry over spilt milk. Let's just clean it up.

word: don't judge a book by its cover
meaning: You should not judge the value or character of someone or something just by looking at the outside.
example: The restaurant looks shabby, but the food is excellent. Don't judge a book by its cover.

word: don't put all your eggs in one basket
meaning: Do not risk everything on a single plan or idea.
example: Apply to several schools; don't put all your eggs in one basket.

word: don't put the cart before the horse
meaning: Do not do things in the wrong order.
example: Let's learn the alphabet before trying to read. Don't put the cart before the horse.

word: early to bed and early to rise, makes a man healthy, wealthy, and wise
meaning: Going to sleep early and waking up early brings success and good health.
example: I try to sleep by 9 PM because early to bed and early to rise makes a man healthy, wealthy, and wise.

word: easy come, easy go
meaning: Things that are acquired easily may be lost just as easily.
example: He won money at the casino and spent it all the next day. Easy come, easy go.

word: every cloud has a silver lining
meaning: There is a positive aspect to every bad or difficult situation.
example: I lost my job, but every cloud has a silver lining: I now have time to start my own business.

word: every dog has its day
meaning: Everyone will be successful or lucky at some point in their life.
example: He finally got the promotion he deserved. Every dog has its day.

word: familiarity breeds contempt
meaning: Knowing someone or something very well can cause you to lose respect for them.
example: They used to be best friends, but living together ruined it. Familiarity breeds contempt.

word: fools rush in where angels fear to tread
meaning: Foolish people are often reckless and act hastily in situations that wiser people avoid.
example: He invested all his money in that risky business without researching. Fools rush in where angels fear to tread.

word: fortune favours the bold
meaning: People who take brave risks are often rewarded.
example: You should ask for that promotion. Fortune favours the bold.

word: god helps those who help themselves
meaning: You should not just wait for good things to happen; you must work hard to achieve them.
example: You need to study if you want to pass the test. God helps those who help themselves.

word: good things come to those who wait
meaning: Patience is often rewarded.
example: I waited in line for hours, but the concert was amazing. Good things come to those who wait.

word: half a loaf is better than none
meaning: It is better to get less than what you want than to get nothing at all.
example: I didn't get the full raise I asked for, but half a loaf is better than none.

word: haste makes waste
meaning: Doing things too quickly often leads to mistakes and poor results.
example: Take your time with the painting; haste makes waste.

word: he who laughs last laughs longest
meaning: The person who is ultimately successful will have the greatest satisfaction.
example: They mocked his invention, but he became a millionaire. He who laughs last laughs longest.

word: honesty is the best policy
meaning: It is always better to tell the truth.
example: Tell your teacher you lost your homework. Honesty is the best policy.

word: hope for the best, but prepare for the worst
meaning: Be optimistic but also make plans in case things go wrong.
example: We brought an umbrella just in case. Hope for the best, but prepare for the worst.

word: if it ain't broke, don't fix it
meaning: Do not try to change or improve something that is already working well.
example: The old washing machine still works perfectly. If it ain't broke, don't fix it.

word: if you can't beat them, join them
meaning: If you cannot defeat your opponents, it might be better to cooperate with them.
example: Since all my friends are playing that new game, I decided to play it too. If you can't beat them, join them.

word: ignorance is bliss
meaning: Not knowing about a problem can sometimes make you happier than knowing about it.
example: I didn't know the cake had so many calories until after I ate it. Ignorance is bliss.

word: it takes two to tango
meaning: Both parties involved in a situation or argument are equally responsible for it.
example: She blamed him for the argument, but it takes two to tango.

word: knowledge is power
meaning: The more you know, the more control you have over your life and situations.
example: Read as many books as you can, because knowledge is power.

word: laughter is the best medicine
meaning: Laughing can help you feel better, both physically and emotionally.
example: Watching a funny movie helped him forget his worries. Laughter is the best medicine.

word: let sleeping dogs lie
meaning: Avoid interfering in a situation that could cause trouble if disturbed.
example: Don't bring up the argument again; it's better to let sleeping dogs lie.

word: lightning never strikes twice in the same place
meaning: It is highly unlikely that a rare or unusual event will happen to the same person or in the same place again.
example: I got a flat tyre yesterday, but don't worry, lightning never strikes twice in the same place.

word: look before you leap
meaning: Think carefully about the possible consequences before you take an action.
example: Before you buy that expensive phone, look before you leap.

word: make hay while the sun shines
meaning: Take advantage of a good opportunity while it lasts.
example: We have a long weekend, so let's get all the cleaning done. Make hay while the sun shines.

word: many hands make light work
meaning: When many people help, a difficult task becomes much easier.
example: If we all help clean the kitchen, it will take five minutes. Many hands make light work.

word: money doesn't grow on trees
meaning: Money is valuable and hard to earn, so it should not be wasted.
example: You cannot buy every toy you see; money doesn't grow on trees.

word: necessity is the mother of invention
meaning: When you really need to solve a problem, you will find a creative way to do it.
example: When we ran out of milk, I used yogurt for the recipe. Necessity is the mother of invention.

word: never look a gift horse in the mouth
meaning: When you receive a gift, do not be ungrateful or look for flaws in it.
example: I didn't really like the sweater she gave me, but never look a gift horse in the mouth.

word: never put off until tomorrow what you can do today
meaning: Do not delay doing tasks that you are able to complete right now.
example: Let's finish the project now. Never put off until tomorrow what you can do today.

word: no news is good news
meaning: If you have not heard any information, it probably means that nothing bad has happened.
example: The doctor hasn't called with the test results yet, but no news is good news.

word: once bitten, twice shy
meaning: If you have a bad experience, you will be more cautious in the future.
example: Since I fell off that bike, I don't want to ride one again. Once bitten, twice shy.

word: one man's trash is another man's treasure
meaning: What one person considers useless, another person might consider valuable.
example: I bought this old chair at a garage sale and fixed it up. One man's trash is another man's treasure.

word: out of sight, out of mind
meaning: If you do not see someone or something frequently, you will eventually forget about them.
example: I haven't thought about my old toys in years. Out of sight, out of mind.

word: patience is a virtue
meaning: The ability to wait calmly is a good quality.
example: We will have dinner soon, just wait. Patience is a virtue.

word: penny wise and pound foolish
meaning: Being careful with small amounts of money but wasteful with large amounts.
example: He drives miles to save a few cents on petrol, but buys a new car every year. He is penny wise and pound foolish.

word: people who live in glass houses shouldn't throw stones
meaning: You should not criticize others for having the same faults that you have.
example: She called him lazy, but she never does her chores. People who live in glass houses shouldn't throw stones.

word: practice makes perfect
meaning: Doing something repeatedly is the only way to become very good at it.
example: Keep practicing the piano; practice makes perfect.

word: pride goes before a fall
meaning: If you are too arrogant or confident, you will eventually make a mistake and fail.
example: He boasted that he couldn't lose the match, but then he did. Pride goes before a fall.

word: rome wasn't built in a day
meaning: Important and great things take a long time to complete.
example: Don't be discouraged that you haven't mastered French yet. Rome wasn't built in a day.

word: silence is golden
meaning: It is often better to say nothing at all.
example: Sometimes during an argument, silence is golden.

word: slow and steady wins the race
meaning: Consistent, effective effort brings success.
example: Take your time with your homework. Slow and steady wins the race.

word: still waters run deep
meaning: People who are quiet and do not show their feelings often have a complex and interesting personality.
example: She doesn't say much in class, but her essays are brilliant. Still waters run deep.

word: strike while the iron is hot
meaning: Take advantage of an opportunity immediately, while it is still available.
example: The tickets are on sale now, so we should strike while the iron is hot.

word: the early bird catches the worm
meaning: The person who takes action earliest will be the most successful.
example: We should arrive at the theme park early to avoid the queues. The early bird catches the worm.

word: the end justifies the means
meaning: A good outcome is worth whatever wrong actions were taken to achieve it.
example: Some believe that using deception to catch a criminal is acceptable because the end justifies the means.

word: the grass is always greener on the other side of the fence
meaning: Other people's lives or situations always seem better than your own.
example: He wishes he had my job, and I wish I had his. The grass is always greener on the other side of the fence.

word: the pen is mightier than the sword
meaning: Writing and ideas are more powerful than physical force or violence.
example: The author changed the world with his book. Truly, the pen is mightier than the sword.

word: the squeaky wheel gets the grease
meaning: The person who complains or speaks up the most is the one who will get attention and help.
example: If you want the broken window fixed, you need to complain to the landlord. The squeaky wheel gets the grease.

word: there's no place like home
meaning: Your own home is the most comfortable and best place to be.
example: The vacation was great, but there's no place like home.

word: there is no time like the present
meaning: Right now is the best time to do something, so do not delay.
example: Why wait until tomorrow to start exercising? There is no time like the present.

word: time is money
meaning: Time is valuable, so it should not be wasted.
example: Please finish the task quickly; time is money.

word: time and tide wait for no man
meaning: You cannot stop the passing of time, so you must use it wisely.
example: We need to leave now if we want to catch the train. Time and tide wait for no man.

word: too many cooks spoil the broth
meaning: If too many people try to manage or work on the same project, it will be ruined.
example: Let's just have one person design the poster. Too many cooks spoil the broth.

word: two heads are better than one
meaning: It is easier to solve a problem if two people work together.
example: Let's study together; two heads are better than one.

word: two wrongs don't make a right
meaning: Doing something bad as revenge for someone else doing something bad to you will not fix the situation.
example: Just because he pushed you doesn't mean you should push him back. Two wrongs don't make a right.

word: what goes around comes around
meaning: The way you treat others will eventually be the way you are treated.
example: He was always unkind, and now no one will help him. What goes around comes around.

word: when in Rome, do as the Romans do
meaning: When you are visiting a new place, you should follow the local customs and behaviour.
example: They eat dinner very late here, so we will too. When in Rome, do as the Romans do.

word: where there's a will, there's a way
meaning: If you are determined enough to do something, you will find a way to achieve it.
example: It will be hard to climb the mountain, but where there's a will, there's a way.

word: you can lead a horse to water, but you can't make it drink
meaning: You can offer someone a good opportunity, but you cannot force them to accept it.
example: I gave him the books to study, but he won't open them. You can lead a horse to water, but you can't make it drink.

word: you can't have your cake and eat it too
meaning: You cannot have the best of two conflicting options.
example: You want to spend all your money but also save for a car. You can't have your cake and eat it too.

word: you can't judge a book by its cover
meaning: You should not form an opinion about something based on its outward appearance.
example: He looks very tough, but he is actually very gentle. You can't judge a book by its cover.

word: you can't make an omelette without breaking eggs
meaning: It is impossible to achieve something important without causing some unpleasant effects.
example: We have to tear down the old building to build the new hospital. You can't make an omelette without breaking eggs.

word: you can't teach an old dog new tricks
meaning: It is very difficult to teach someone who is set in their ways a new way of doing things.
example: My grandfather refuses to learn how to use a smartphone. You can't teach an old dog new tricks.

word: a friend to all is a friend to none
meaning: Someone who tries to be everyone's friend is not truly loyal to anyone.
example: He agrees with whatever anyone says, but a friend to all is a friend to none.

word: a guilty conscience needs no accuser
meaning: If you have done something wrong, you will feel guilty even if no one accuses you.
example: He confessed before anyone even suspected him; a guilty conscience needs no accuser.

word: a house divided against itself cannot stand
meaning: An organization or family that is fighting internally will fail.
example: We must stop arguing and work as a team, because a house divided against itself cannot stand.

word: a penny saved is a penny earned
meaning: It is as useful to save money that you already have as it is to earn more money.
example: Instead of buying coffee, I bring it from home. A penny saved is a penny earned.

word: a rising tide lifts all boats
meaning: When the general economy or situation improves, everyone benefits.
example: The new factory brought prosperity to the whole town; a rising tide lifts all boats.

word: all work and no play makes jack a dull boy
meaning: Without time off from work, a person becomes bored and boring.
example: You need to take a break from studying. All work and no play makes Jack a dull boy.

word: always put your best foot forward
meaning: Try to act as well as possible and make a good impression.
example: For your job interview, make sure you dress well and always put your best foot forward.

word: an army marches on its stomach
meaning: To be effective, people need to be well-fed.
example: We must provide plenty of food for the volunteers today; an army marches on its stomach.

word: april showers bring may flowers
meaning: Unpleasant things can sometimes lead to good things.
example: The heavy rain ruined our picnic, but April showers bring May flowers.

word: bad news travels fast
meaning: People are quick to talk about bad things that happen.
example: Everyone knew about the accident within minutes because bad news travels fast.

word: blood will tell
meaning: A person's background or true character will eventually show itself.
example: He tries to hide his temper, but eventually blood will tell.

word: boys will be boys
meaning: Used to express the view that mischievous or childish behaviour is typical of boys.
example: They came home covered in mud, but boys will be boys.

word: carpe diem
meaning: Used to urge someone to make the most of the present time and give little thought to the future.
example: Don't worry about tomorrow, carpe diem!

word: charity begins at home
meaning: You should take care of your own family and friends before helping others.
example: Before donating to foreign causes, remember that charity begins at home.

word: children should be seen and not heard
meaning: Children should behave quietly and politely in the presence of adults.
example: In my grandfather's day, they believed children should be seen and not heard.

word: confession is good for the soul
meaning: Admitting your mistakes or wrongdoings makes you feel better.
example: Tell your teacher you cheated; confession is good for the soul.

word: dead men tell no tales
meaning: Someone who is dead cannot reveal secrets.
example: The pirates made sure no witnesses survived, because dead men tell no tales.

word: discretion is the better part of valour
meaning: It is better to be careful and avoid unnecessary risks than to be needlessly brave.
example: He decided not to fight the larger boy, figuring discretion is the better part of valour.

word: don't throw the baby out with the bathwater
meaning: Do not get rid of good or valuable things while getting rid of bad ones.
example: The new manager changed the terrible rules but kept the good staff. He didn't throw the baby out with the bathwater.

word: east or west, home is best
meaning: Home is the best place to be, no matter where else you travel.
example: I had a great time in Europe, but East or West, home is best.

word: even a stopped clock is right twice a day
meaning: Even someone who is usually wrong can sometimes be right by chance.
example: He guessed the answer correctly, but even a stopped clock is right twice a day.

word: every man for himself
meaning: Everyone must look after their own interests.
example: When the ship started sinking, it was every man for himself.

word: everything comes to him who waits
meaning: Patient people will eventually get what they want.
example: She finally got the job she wanted after years of trying. Everything comes to him who waits.

word: experience is the best teacher
meaning: You learn more from doing things yourself than from being told about them.
example: You can read about driving all you want, but experience is the best teacher.

word: fact is stranger than fiction
meaning: Real events are sometimes more unusual or surprising than invented stories.
example: The story of his life is so incredible, proving that fact is stranger than fiction.

word: fine feathers make fine birds
meaning: People are judged by the clothes they wear.
example: She bought an expensive suit for the interview because fine feathers make fine birds.

word: first come, first served
meaning: Those who arrive first will receive what they want before those who arrive later.
example: We can't reserve seats; it's strictly first come, first served.

word: fish and guests smell after three days
meaning: Visitors begin to become annoying if they stay too long.
example: My cousin has been staying with us for a week now, and fish and guests smell after three days.

word: flattery will get you nowhere
meaning: Paying compliments will not help you get what you want.
example: Telling me I look nice won't change my mind. Flattery will get you nowhere.

word: forgive and forget
meaning: Pardon someone for a wrong and put it out of your mind completely.
example: We had a fight, but we decided to forgive and forget.

word: good fences make good neighbours
meaning: Respecting each other's privacy and boundaries leads to good relationships.
example: We built a wall between our gardens, because good fences make good neighbours.

word: great minds think alike
meaning: Intelligent people often have the same ideas at the same time.
example: We both bought the same gift for her; great minds think alike.

word: half the truth is often a whole lie
meaning: Leaving out important facts can be just as misleading as telling a direct lie.
example: He didn't tell us the car had broken down before. Half the truth is often a whole lie.

word: hard cases make bad law
meaning: Extreme or unusual cases should not be the basis for general rules or laws.
example: The judge shouldn't change the rule for one unusual situation; hard cases make bad law.

word: he who hesitates is lost
meaning: If you delay taking action, you will lose your opportunity.
example: You need to accept the offer now before someone else does. He who hesitates is lost.

word: history repeats itself
meaning: The same kinds of events seem to happen over and over again.
example: Another economic crash is happening; it seems history repeats itself.

word: home is where the heart is
meaning: Your true home is with the people you love, regardless of where you are.
example: We live in a small apartment now, but home is where the heart is.

word: hope springs eternal
meaning: People always continue to hope, even in bad situations.
example: He buys a lottery ticket every week. Hope springs eternal.

word: hunger is the best sauce
meaning: Everything tastes better when you are very hungry.
example: After a long hike, even plain bread tastes amazing. Hunger is the best sauce.

word: if the shoe fits, wear it
meaning: If a criticism or description applies to you, you should accept it.
example: I didn't say you were lazy, but if the shoe fits, wear it.

word: if wishes were horses, beggars would ride
meaning: If it were easy to get what you want just by wishing for it, everyone would have everything.
example: I wish I were rich, but if wishes were horses, beggars would ride.

word: imitation is the sincerest form of flattery
meaning: Copying someone is the highest compliment you can pay them.
example: She copied my hairstyle, but I don't mind. Imitation is the sincerest form of flattery.

word: in for a penny, in for a pound
meaning: Since you have started something, you should see it through to the end, no matter the cost or effort.
example: We've already spent so much time on this project, we might as well finish it. In for a penny, in for a pound.

word: it is always darkest before the dawn
meaning: Things often seem their worst just before they start to get better.
example: Don't give up on the business yet; it is always darkest before the dawn.

word: it is better to give than to receive
meaning: It is more rewarding to give things to others than to get things for yourself.
example: Donating to charity made her realise it is better to give than to receive.

word: it never rains but it pours
meaning: Bad things often happen at the same time.
example: First I lost my keys, then my car broke down. It never rains but it pours.

word: it takes a village to raise a child
meaning: Raising a child involves the whole community, not just the parents.
example: The neighbours all help look after the kids because it takes a village to raise a child.

word: it takes all sorts to make a world
meaning: People are very different from each other, and that is a normal part of life.
example: I don't understand his strange hobbies, but it takes all sorts to make a world.

word: jack of all trades, master of none
meaning: Someone who is competent in many skills is not outstanding in any single one.
example: He can fix a pipe and paint a wall, but he is a Jack of all trades, master of none.

word: keep your friends close, and your enemies closer
meaning: You should be more observant of your enemies than of your friends.
example: He invited his rival to the party, figuring he should keep his friends close, and his enemies closer.

word: leave well enough alone
meaning: Do not try to change something that is already satisfactory, as you might make it worse.
example: The essay is good as it is; don't rewrite it. Leave well enough alone.

word: let bygones be bygones
meaning: To forgive and forget past disagreements.
example: They decided to end their feud and let bygones be bygones.

word: let the buyer beware
meaning: The buyer is responsible for checking the quality of goods before purchasing.
example: You bought a used car without checking the engine. Let the buyer beware.

word: life is what you make it
meaning: Your life will be whatever you choose to shape it into through your actions.
example: You have to work hard for your dreams because life is what you make it.

word: like father, like son
meaning: Fathers and sons often resemble each other in behaviour or character.
example: He loves fixing cars just like his dad; like father, like son.

word: live and let live
meaning: You should tolerate the opinions and behaviour of others so they will tolerate yours.
example: We disagree on politics, but we live and let live.

word: love is blind
meaning: If you love someone, you cannot see any faults in that person.
example: She ignores his terrible temper because love is blind.

word: love makes the world go round
meaning: Love is the principal force behind human life.
example: Seeing them care for each other proves that love makes the world go round.

word: make a virtue of necessity
meaning: To make the best of a difficult or unavoidable situation.
example: Since it rained, we made a virtue of necessity and had a fun indoor picnic.

word: man does not live by bread alone
meaning: People need more than just physical things like food to live a fulfilling life; they need spiritual or mental nourishment too.
example: Art and music are important because man does not live by bread alone.

word: manners maketh man
meaning: Polite behaviour indicates a person of good character.
example: Always say please and thank you; manners maketh man.

word: many a true word is spoken in jest
meaning: Things that are said as jokes often contain a lot of truth.
example: He joked about quitting, but many a true word is spoken in jest.

word: marry in haste, repent at leisure
meaning: If you marry too quickly, you will have a long time to regret it.
example: They wed after knowing each other for a week. Marry in haste, repent at leisure.

word: might is right
meaning: Those who are powerful can do what they want because no one can stop them.
example: The stronger country simply took the territory, proving that might is right.

word: misery loves company
meaning: People who are unhappy like to share their troubles with others.
example: He complained all day and wanted everyone else to be sad too; misery loves company.

word: money is the root of all evil
meaning: The love of money causes people to do bad things.
example: People steal and lie to get rich; money is the root of all evil.

word: money talks
meaning: Wealth gives power and influence to those who possess it.
example: He got the best table in the restaurant with a large tip; money talks.

word: more haste, less speed
meaning: If you try to do things too quickly, it will take you longer because you will make mistakes.
example: I rushed typing the report and had to redo it. More haste, less speed.

word: needs must when the devil drives
meaning: Sometimes people are forced by circumstances to do things they would rather not do.
example: I had to take the terrible job to pay my rent; needs must when the devil drives.

word: never judge by appearances
meaning: Do not form an opinion based only on how someone or something looks.
example: The old car ran perfectly, teaching me to never judge by appearances.

word: never say die
meaning: Do not give up hope, even in a difficult situation.
example: We are losing the game, but keep trying! Never say die!

word: no pain, no gain
meaning: You must work hard and face difficulties to achieve success.
example: My muscles hurt from training, but no pain, no gain.

word: nothing ventured, nothing gained
meaning: You cannot achieve anything if you do not take risks.
example: I'm nervous about asking her out, but nothing ventured, nothing gained.

word: old habits die hard
meaning: It is very difficult to change an established pattern of behaviour.
example: He still wakes up at 5 am even though he retired; old habits die hard.

word: once a thief, always a thief
meaning: People believe that someone who has committed a crime will never change.
example: No one trusted him after he stole the money. Once a thief, always a thief.

word: one good turn deserves another
meaning: If someone does you a favour, you should do a favour for them in return.
example: You helped me move, so I will buy you dinner. One good turn deserves another.

word: one man's meat is another man's poison
meaning: What is good or enjoyable for one person may be distasteful for someone else.
example: I love spicy food, but it makes him sick. One man's meat is another man's poison.

word: one swallow does not make a summer
meaning: A single positive event does not mean that everything is going to be good.
example: We won one match, but we are still at the bottom of the league. One swallow does not make a summer.

word: opposites attract
meaning: People who are very different from each other are often attracted to each other.
example: He is quiet and she is very loud, but opposites attract.

word: out of the frying pan into the fire
meaning: Moving from a bad situation into a much worse one.
example: He quit his stressful job only to start one with an even worse boss; out of the frying pan into the fire.

word: pay the piper
meaning: Bear the consequences of an action or activity that you have enjoyed.
example: We partied all weekend, and now on Monday we must pay the piper.

word: pitchers have ears
meaning: Used to warn that people (especially children) may be listening.
example: Don't talk about the surprise party now; pitchers have ears.

word: possession is nine-tenths of the law
meaning: Having physical control of property is the most important advantage in a dispute over ownership.
example: I found the lost watch and I am keeping it; possession is nine-tenths of the law.

word: power corrupts; absolute power corrupts absolutely
meaning: When people get power, they tend to behave immorally, and more power makes them worse.
example: The dictator became a tyrant once he seized control. Power corrupts; absolute power corrupts absolutely.

word: procrastination is the thief of time
meaning: Delaying doing things wastes your valuable time.
example: Stop watching TV and start your homework; procrastination is the thief of time.

word: rules are made to be broken
meaning: Sometimes it is necessary or acceptable to ignore a rule.
example: We aren't supposed to eat in here, but rules are made to be broken.

word: seeing is believing
meaning: You need to see something with your own eyes before you can believe it exists or happens.
example: I didn't think he could juggle five balls, but seeing is believing.

word: snitches get stitches
meaning: People who report others to the authorities will be punished or physically harmed by those they betrayed.
example: He refused to tell the police who started the fight, knowing that snitches get stitches.

word: spare the rod and spoil the child
meaning: If you do not punish a child when they do wrong, they will become badly behaved.
example: His parents never discipline him, and he is a terror. Spare the rod and spoil the child.

word: speak of the devil and he doth appear
meaning: Said when a person appears just after being mentioned.
example: We were just talking about you! Speak of the devil and he doth appear.

word: sticks and stones may break my bones, but words will never hurt me
meaning: Physical attacks can cause real injury, but unkind words cannot cause physical harm.
example: Ignore their name-calling; sticks and stones may break my bones, but words will never hurt me.

word: success has many fathers, but failure is an orphan
meaning: People are eager to take credit for a success, but no one wants to take responsibility for a failure.
example: When the project succeeded everyone claimed they helped, proving success has many fathers, but failure is an orphan.

word: the apple doesn't fall far from the tree
meaning: Children often resemble their parents in character and behaviour.
example: He is a talented musician just like his mother. The apple doesn't fall far from the tree.

word: the best is yet to come
meaning: The most positive or exciting things will happen in the future.
example: We had a great year, but the best is yet to come.

word: the bigger they are, the harder they fall
meaning: The more powerful or important someone is, the more severely they will suffer when they fail.
example: The champion was undefeated until tonight; the bigger they are, the harder they fall.

word: the boy is father to the man
meaning: A person's character is formed when they are a child.
example: He was always fixing things as a kid and now he is an engineer. The boy is father to the man.

word: there is honor among thieves
meaning: Even corrupt or bad people have certain standards of behaviour that they respect.
example: The criminals refused to betray each other, showing there is honor among thieves.

word: two's company, three's a crowd
meaning: Two people together is a good situation, but a third person ruins it.
example: I'll leave you two alone for your date. Two's company, three's a crowd.

word: variety is the spice of life
meaning: New and different experiences make life interesting.
example: I try a new restaurant every week because variety is the spice of life.

word: you can't make a silk purse out of a sow's ear
meaning: You cannot make something good or valuable out of poor-quality materials.
example: No matter how much you polish that old bike, you can't make a silk purse out of a sow's ear.

word: a burnt child dreads the fire
meaning: Someone who has experienced something bad will be very careful to avoid it in the future.
example: He refuses to invest in stocks after losing money last year; a burnt child dreads the fire.

word: a cat may look at a king
meaning: An inferior has certain rights, even in the presence of a superior.
example: I have every right to attend the public meeting, even if the mayor is there; a cat may look at a king.

word: a closed mouth catches no flies
meaning: If you don't speak, you won't say anything foolish or get into trouble.
example: He stayed silent during the argument, knowing that a closed mouth catches no flies.

word: a coward dies a thousand times before his death
meaning: People who are afraid constantly suffer from fear, whereas brave people only suffer when they actually die.
example: Stop worrying about the exam and just take it; a coward dies a thousand times before his death.

word: a drowning man will clutch at a straw
meaning: A person in a desperate situation will try anything, no matter how unlikely it is to help.
example: The failing company tried a ridiculous marketing stunt, because a drowning man will clutch at a straw.

word: a fault confessed is half redressed
meaning: Admitting you did something wrong is the first step towards making things right.
example: Tell your mother you broke the vase; a fault confessed is half redressed.

word: a fool's bolt is soon shot
meaning: A foolish person acts too quickly and wastes their resources or opportunities.
example: He spent all his allowance on the first day of the trip; a fool's bolt is soon shot.

word: a golden key can open any door
meaning: Having enough money can solve almost any problem or give you access to anything.
example: He managed to get tickets to the sold-out concert because a golden key can open any door.

word: a good beginning makes a good ending
meaning: Starting a task well increases the chances of finishing it successfully.
example: Make sure you plan your essay carefully first; a good beginning makes a good ending.

word: a good conscience is a soft pillow
meaning: You can sleep peacefully if you know you have done nothing wrong.
example: I told the truth, and I'll sleep well tonight; a good conscience is a soft pillow.

word: a little knowledge is a dangerous thing
meaning: Knowing only a small amount about something can lead you to make bad decisions.
example: He tried to fix the wiring himself and caused a power cut. A little knowledge is a dangerous thing.

word: a man is known by the company he keeps
meaning: People will judge your character based on the friends you associate with.
example: You shouldn't hang out with the troublemakers; a man is known by the company he keeps.

word: a miss is as good as a mile
meaning: Failing by a small margin is still a failure, just like failing by a large margin.
example: We lost the game by one point, but a miss is as good as a mile.

word: a new broom sweeps clean
meaning: A person newly appointed to a position often makes many changes to improve things.
example: The new headteacher introduced strict rules immediately; a new broom sweeps clean.

word: a nod is as good as a wink to a blind horse
meaning: A subtle hint is completely useless to someone who doesn't understand or isn't paying attention.
example: I tried dropping hints about my birthday, but a nod is as good as a wink to a blind horse.

word: a problem shared is a problem halved
meaning: Talking about a problem with someone else makes it seem less difficult or overwhelming.
example: Tell me what's worrying you; a problem shared is a problem halved.

word: a rotten apple spoils the barrel
meaning: One bad person in a group can negatively influence everyone else.
example: The team was great until one lazy player joined. A rotten apple spoils the barrel.

word: a smooth sea never made a skilled sailor
meaning: Difficult experiences are necessary to build character and learn important skills.
example: The tough project taught her a lot; a smooth sea never made a skilled sailor.

word: a word to the wise is sufficient
meaning: Intelligent people only need a brief warning or piece of advice to understand what to do.
example: I won't say any more about the danger. A word to the wise is sufficient.

word: after a storm comes a calm
meaning: Things will eventually get better after a period of difficulty or trouble.
example: The argument was fierce, but after a storm comes a calm.

word: age before beauty
meaning: A playful phrase used to allow an older person to go first.
example: Please, you go through the door first; age before beauty.

word: all roads lead to rome
meaning: There are many different ways to achieve the same goal.
example: You study in the morning, I study at night, but we both pass; all roads lead to Rome.

word: all's fair in love and war
meaning: In certain highly competitive situations, rules of fair play do not apply.
example: He sabotaged his rival's date, claiming that all's fair in love and war.

word: any port in a storm
meaning: In a difficult situation, any solution or source of help is acceptable.
example: I don't love this cheap motel, but it's late and we need sleep; any port in a storm.

word: appearances can be deceptive
meaning: Things are not always as they seem on the outside.
example: The fierce-looking dog was actually very friendly. Appearances can be deceptive.

word: as you make your bed, so you must lie upon it
meaning: You must accept the consequences of your own actions.
example: You didn't study for the test, so accept your failing grade. As you make your bed, so you must lie upon it.

word: ask no questions and hear no lies
meaning: It is sometimes better not to ask about something if you suspect the answer will be dishonest.
example: I didn't ask where he got the money; ask no questions and hear no lies.

word: beware of greeks bearing gifts
meaning: Do not trust enemies even when they appear friendly or offer gifts.
example: My rival company suddenly offered to help me; beware of Greeks bearing gifts.

word: better the devil you know than the devil you don't
meaning: It is better to deal with a difficult person or situation that you know than a new one that might be worse.
example: I dislike my boss, but I won't change jobs. Better the devil you know than the devil you don't.

word: beware of a silent dog and still water
meaning: People who are quiet or seem calm can sometimes be dangerous or deceptive.
example: He never argues, but he plots against us; beware of a silent dog and still water.

word: by learning you will teach, by teaching you will learn
meaning: Teaching others helps you to understand the subject better yourself.
example: Tutoring younger students improved my own grades; by learning you will teach, by teaching you will learn.

word: call a spade a spade
meaning: To speak honestly and directly about something, even if it is unpleasant.
example: Let's call a spade a spade; the project failed because we were lazy.

word: catch not at the shadow and lose the substance
meaning: Do not pursue something unrealistic and lose what you actually have.
example: He quit a good job for a risky startup that failed. Catch not at the shadow and lose the substance.

word: charity covers a multitude of sins
meaning: Being kind and generous to others can make up for many of your own faults.
example: He can be rude, but he donates so much to the hospital; charity covers a multitude of sins.

word: clothes do not make the man
meaning: A person's character cannot be judged by how they dress.
example: He was wearing old jeans but was a brilliant scientist. Clothes do not make the man.

word: comparisons are odious
meaning: It is unpleasant and unhelpful to compare different people or things.
example: Stop comparing your child's grades to others; comparisons are odious.

word: constant dropping wears away a stone
meaning: Persistence and continuous effort can achieve great results over time.
example: Keep practising your instrument every day; constant dropping wears away a stone.

word: cut your coat according to your cloth
meaning: You should limit your plans and spending to what you can afford.
example: We can't afford a large wedding right now; we must cut our coat according to our cloth.

word: damned if you do, damned if you don't
meaning: A situation where you will be criticized no matter what choice you make.
example: If I help, they say I'm interfering; if I don't, they say I don't care. Damned if you do, damned if you don't.

word: do as i say, not as i do
meaning: Follow my advice, even if I don't follow it myself.
example: The doctor smoked while telling his patient to quit, saying, 'Do as I say, not as I do.'

word: do not wear out your welcome
meaning: Do not visit someone for so long or so often that they become annoyed with you.
example: We should go home now; we do not want to wear out our welcome.

word: dogs bark, but the caravan moves on
meaning: People may criticize you, but you should continue with your plans regardless.
example: Ignore the negative comments on your project; dogs bark, but the caravan moves on.

word: don't change horses in midstream
meaning: Do not alter your plans or leadership when you are in the middle of a difficult task.
example: We shouldn't switch managers halfway through the project. Don't change horses in midstream.

word: don't cross your bridges before you come to them
meaning: Do not worry about future problems until they actually happen.
example: We don't know if it will rain next week, so don't cross your bridges before you come to them.

word: don't cut off your nose to spite your face
meaning: Do not act out of anger in a way that harms you more than anyone else.
example: Refusing to eat dinner just because you're mad at Mom is silly; don't cut off your nose to spite your face.

word: don't put new wine into old bottles
meaning: You should not try to force new ideas or methods into old, rigid systems.
example: Using modern software on that ancient computer won't work; don't put new wine into old bottles.

word: even a worm will turn
meaning: Even the most gentle or timid person will eventually fight back if pushed too far.
example: He took the bullying for years, but today he fought back; even a worm will turn.

word: every family has a skeleton in the cupboard
meaning: Every family has a shameful secret that they try to keep hidden.
example: They seem perfect, but I'm sure they have issues; every family has a skeleton in the cupboard.

word: every man has his price
meaning: Everyone can be persuaded to do something bad if they are offered a large enough reward.
example: The honest judge finally accepted the massive bribe; every man has his price.

word: every picture tells a story
meaning: You can learn a lot about a situation or a person's life by looking at visual evidence.
example: The messy room showed how busy she had been; every picture tells a story.

word: failure is the stepping stone to success
meaning: Making mistakes and failing helps you learn and eventually succeed.
example: Don't be sad about failing the first test; failure is the stepping stone to success.

word: faint heart never won fair lady
meaning: You must be bold and take risks if you want to succeed, especially in romance.
example: Go ahead and ask her out! Faint heart never won fair lady.

word: finders keepers, losers weepers
meaning: A person who finds something gets to keep it, and the person who lost it can only cry.
example: I found this coin on the ground, so finders keepers, losers weepers!

word: fine words butter no parsnips
meaning: Saying nice things does not produce practical results or solve problems.
example: He promised to help but did nothing; fine words butter no parsnips.

word: first impressions are the most lasting
meaning: The initial opinion you form of someone or something is very difficult to change.
example: Make sure you smile on your first day of school; first impressions are the most lasting.

word: fool me once, shame on you; fool me twice, shame on me
meaning: You should learn from being tricked and not let it happen again.
example: I won't lend him money again after he didn't pay me back. Fool me once, shame on you; fool me twice, shame on me.

word: forewarned is forearmed
meaning: Knowing about a danger in advance helps you prepare for it.
example: The weather forecast said it would snow, so I bought salt; forewarned is forearmed.

word: give a dog a bad name and hang him
meaning: Once a person has a bad reputation, it is very hard to get rid of it, and they will be blamed for things.
example: Even though he didn't break the window, they blamed him because of his past. Give a dog a bad name and hang him.

word: give a man a fish and you feed him for a day; teach a man to fish and you feed him for a lifetime
meaning: It is better to teach someone how to do something themselves than to just do it for them.
example: Instead of doing his homework, I showed him how to solve it. Teach a man to fish and you feed him for a lifetime.

word: give credit where credit is due
meaning: You should acknowledge and praise someone's good work or ideas.
example: I don't like her much, but her presentation was excellent; give credit where credit is due.

word: give him an inch and he'll take a mile
meaning: If you allow someone a small concession, they will take advantage of you and demand much more.
example: I let him borrow my pen, and he kept it forever. Give him an inch and he'll take a mile.

word: god tempers the wind to the shorn lamb
meaning: People who are vulnerable or have suffered are protected from further excessive hardship.
example: After she lost her job, she found an unexpectedly cheap apartment. God tempers the wind to the shorn lamb.

word: good things come in small packages
meaning: The size of something does not determine its value or quality.
example: The diamond ring was tiny but beautiful; good things come in small packages.

word: grasp all, lose all
meaning: If you try to get or keep everything, you risk losing everything.
example: He tried to hold four cups at once and dropped them all; grasp all, lose all.

word: great oaks from little acorns grow
meaning: Great things or successful enterprises often start from very small beginnings.
example: Their global company started in a tiny garage; great oaks from little acorns grow.

word: handsome is as handsome does
meaning: A person's true worth is defined by their good actions, not their physical appearance.
example: He isn't very attractive, but he volunteers every weekend; handsome is as handsome does.

word: he who pays the piper calls the tune
meaning: The person who provides the money for something has the right to decide how it is done.
example: My dad is paying for the wedding, so he gets to invite his friends. He who pays the piper calls the tune.

word: health is better than wealth
meaning: Being healthy is more important and valuable than having a lot of money.
example: The billionaire was miserable because he was sick; health is really better than wealth.

word: hindsight is a wonderful thing
meaning: It is easy to know what should have been done after an event has already happened.
example: I shouldn't have bought that expensive car, but hindsight is a wonderful thing.

word: if at first you don't succeed, try, try again
meaning: You should not give up after failing, but keep trying until you succeed.
example: He failed his driving test, but if at first you don't succeed, try, try again.

word: if you play with fire, you'll get burned
meaning: If you take dangerous risks, you will eventually suffer the consequences.
example: He kept driving too fast, and finally crashed. If you play with fire, you'll get burned.

word: if you want a thing done well, do it yourself
meaning: You cannot always rely on others to do a good job, so sometimes you must do it.
example: The cleaners missed a spot, so I cleaned it. If you want a thing done well, do it yourself.

word: ignorance of the law is no excuse
meaning: Not knowing that something is illegal does not prevent you from being punished for doing it.
example: I didn't know the speed limit was lower here, but ignorance of the law is no excuse.

word: ill gotten gains never prosper
meaning: Money or property acquired dishonestly will not bring true happiness or success.
example: The thief was eventually caught and lost everything; ill gotten gains never prosper.

word: it is easy to be wise after the event
meaning: It is easy to understand what went wrong after a failure has happened.
example: We know now that the bridge was weak, but it is easy to be wise after the event.

word: it's no use locking the stable door after the horse has bolted
meaning: Taking preventative measures after the damage has already been done is pointless.
example: Installing an alarm after you were robbed is useless; it's no use locking the stable door after the horse has bolted.

word: it takes two to make a quarrel
meaning: An argument requires two people, and both are usually to blame.
example: Don't just blame your brother for the fight; it takes two to make a quarrel.

word: keep your mouth shut and your eyes open
meaning: You will learn more by observing silently than by talking.
example: On your first day at the new job, keep your mouth shut and your eyes open.

word: laugh and the world laughs with you, weep and you weep alone
meaning: People prefer to be around happy people and tend to avoid those who are sad.
example: When I won the lottery everyone wanted to be my friend, but when I was broke no one called. Laugh and the world laughs with you, weep and you weep alone.

word: least said, soonest mended
meaning: The less you talk about a problem or argument, the quicker it will be resolved or forgotten.
example: I won't bring up the mistake again; least said, soonest mended.

word: let the punishment fit the crime
meaning: The severity of a punishment should match the seriousness of the offence.
example: Grounding him for a month just for being late seems unfair; let the punishment fit the crime.

word: little strokes fell great oaks
meaning: Steady, continuous effort can accomplish a large or difficult task.
example: She wrote the novel one page a day; little strokes fell great oaks.

word: lookers-on see most of the game
meaning: People who observe a situation from the outside often understand it better than those involved.
example: As a consultant, I can spot their mistakes easily; lookers-on see most of the game.

word: man proposes, god disposes
meaning: People can make plans, but their success ultimately depends on factors beyond their control.
example: We planned an outdoor wedding, but a hurricane hit. Man proposes, God disposes.

word: misfortunes never come singly
meaning: Bad things tend to happen at the same time or follow one another quickly.
example: First my car broke down, then I lost my job. Misfortunes never come singly.

word: need makes the old wife trot
meaning: Necessity forces people to do things they would not normally do or be capable of.
example: He hated running, but he sprinted to catch the last train; need makes the old wife trot.

word: never trouble trouble till trouble troubles you
meaning: Do not worry about or seek out problems before they actually happen.
example: Stop worrying about next year's exams; never trouble trouble till trouble troubles you.

word: no cross, no crown
meaning: You cannot achieve success or glory without suffering or hard work.
example: The athlete trained for years in pain to win gold; no cross, no crown.

word: nothing succeeds like success
meaning: Achieving something successful gives you confidence and resources to achieve even more.
example: After his first hit movie, everyone wanted to hire him; nothing succeeds like success.

word: out of debt, out of danger
meaning: If you do not owe money, you are safe from financial ruin.
example: I finally paid off my loan and feel so relieved; out of debt, out of danger.

word: out of the mouths of babes
meaning: Children sometimes say surprisingly wise or perceptive things.
example: The five-year-old gave the best advice on being kind; out of the mouths of babes.

word: practice what you preach
meaning: You should behave in the way that you advise other people to behave.
example: The doctor who told me to diet needs to practice what he preaches.

word: prevention is better than cure
meaning: It is better to stop a problem from happening than to try to fix it later.
example: Getting a vaccine is smart because prevention is better than cure.

word: put your money where your mouth is
meaning: Back up your words or boasts with action or money.
example: If you're so sure you will win the race, bet on it! Put your money where your mouth is.

word: saying is one thing and doing is another
meaning: It is much easier to talk about doing something than to actually do it.
example: He promised to clean the whole house, but saying is one thing and doing is another.

word: so many men, so many opinions
meaning: Everyone has their own different point of view.
example: We couldn't agree on a restaurant because so many men, so many opinions.

word: step by step goes a long way
meaning: Taking small, steady actions will eventually lead to significant progress.
example: Saving a few dollars every week added up over the year; step by step goes a long way.

word: the exception proves the rule
meaning: The fact that an exception exists demonstrates that a general rule applies in most cases.
example: The store is usually closed on Sundays, so seeing it open today is the exception that proves the rule.

word: the proof of the pudding is in the eating
meaning: You can only judge the true quality of something by experiencing or testing it.
example: The new car looks great, but the proof of the pudding is in the eating.

word: time heals all wounds
meaning: Feelings of hurt, grief, or anger will eventually fade as time passes.
example: She was devastated by the breakup, but time heals all wounds.
